authors.name: Filastrius of Brescia
authors.bio: Bishop of Brescia in northern Italy. Author of the earliest surviving Latin work devoted exclusively to cataloging heresies.

authors.name: Hippolytus of Rome
authors.bio: Roman presbyter and theologian. His Refutation of All Heresies is one of the most detailed surviving accounts of Gnostic theology.

authors.name: Irenaeus of Lyon
authors.bio: Bishop of Lyon. His Against Heresies is the earliest systematic refutation of Gnostic theology and the source of the famous formulation: God became man so that man might become God.

authors.name: Tertullian
authors.bio: Carthaginian theologian, one of the most vivid prose stylists in Christian Latin. Later joined the Montanist movement.

authors.name: Marius Victorinus
authors.bio: Neoplatonist philosopher and rhetorician who converted to Christianity in old age. His theological works fuse Platonic metaphysics with Trinitarian doctrine.

authors.name: Sin-leqi-unninni
authors.bio: Babylonian scribe traditionally credited with compiling the Standard Babylonian version of the Epic of Gilgamesh from older Sumerian sources. The most important literary editor in recorded history.

authors.name: Peter (attributed)
authors.bio: 2 Peter is traditionally attributed to the Apostle Peter but widely considered pseudepigraphal by modern scholars, likely composed in the early second century. It contains the most explicit theosis statement in the New Testament.

authors.name: Athanasius of Alexandria
authors.bio: Bishop of Alexandria and the foremost defender of Nicene orthodoxy against Arianism. Author of the most famous theosis formula in Christian history: “He became man so that we might become God.”

authors.name: John (attributed)
authors.bio: The Gospel of John, traditionally attributed to the Apostle John, contains the most developed theology of the divine nature of Christ in the New Testament. The Prologue (1:1-18) is the foundational text for Logos theology.

authors.name: Matthew (attributed)
authors.bio: The Gospel of Matthew, traditionally attributed to the apostle Matthew, contains the Sermon on the Mount and the command to "be perfect as your heavenly Father is perfect" — a key theosis text.

authors.name: Mark (attributed)
authors.bio: The Gospel of Mark is the earliest of the four canonical Gospels. It opens with no birth narrative, proceeding directly to John the Baptist and the baptism of Jesus — the moment the Spirit descends on flesh.

authors.name: Luke (attributed)
authors.bio: The Gospel of Luke is unique in beginning with a statement of historical methodology. Luke tells Theophilus he has investigated everything carefully from eyewitnesses — the earliest statement of source verification in Christian literature.

authors.name: Council of Nicaea / Constantinople
authors.bio: The ecumenical councils that produced the Nicene Creed, defining Christ as homoousios (of one substance) with the Father.

authors.name: Psalmist (attributed to Asaph)
authors.bio: Psalm 82 is attributed to Asaph. It depicts God standing in the divine assembly, judging the gods. The most important Old Testament text for the theosis debate.

authors.name: Moses (attributed)
authors.bio: Genesis is traditionally attributed to Moses. The creation of humanity in the image and likeness of God (1:26-27) is the foundational text for the theological anthropology that underlies the theosis tradition.

authors.name: Paul (attributed)
authors.bio: The apostle Paul's letters are the earliest Christian documents. His theology of adoption, co-inheritance with Christ, and transformation into the image of the Son provides the scriptural framework for theosis.

authors.name: Author of Hebrews
authors.bio: The Epistle to the Hebrews, of disputed authorship, opens with the most developed Christological statement in the NT epistles.

authors.name: John the Elder (attributed)
authors.bio: The Johannine epistles develop the themes of mutual indwelling and divine likeness found in the Fourth Gospel.

authors.name: Isaiah (attributed)
authors.bio: The prophet Isaiah's throne vision (ch. 6) is the foundational text for the tradition of divine glory and human transformation through encounter with God.

authors.name: Ezekiel (attributed)
authors.bio: Ezekiel's vision of the divine chariot-throne became the foundation of Jewish mystical tradition (merkabah).

authors.name: Daniel (attributed)
authors.bio: The Son of Man vision in Daniel 7 became Jesus's preferred self-designation and a key text for the relationship between human and divine authority.

authors.name: Solomon (attributed)
authors.bio: Proverbs and Ecclesiastes, traditionally attributed to Solomon. Proverbs 8 personifies Wisdom as a pre-existent divine agent.

authors.name: Clement of Alexandria
authors.bio: Head of the catechetical school in Alexandria. The first Christian thinker to systematically engage with Greek philosophy, and an early advocate of theosis through knowledge of God.

authors.name: Origen of Alexandria
authors.bio: The most prolific and controversial of the early Church Fathers. His vision of universal restoration (apokatastasis) pushed the theosis question to its logical extreme.

authors.name: Basil of Caesarea
authors.bio: One of the Cappadocian Fathers. His On the Holy Spirit contains the most explicit statement that the Spirit makes humans God.

authors.name: Gregory of Nazianzus
authors.bio: The Theologian. His Five Theological Orations are the definitive statement of Cappadocian Trinitarian theology.

authors.name: Gregory of Nyssa
authors.bio: The most philosophically sophisticated of the Cappadocian Fathers. Developed the concept of epektasis: endless progress into the infinite God.

authors.name: Maximus the Confessor
authors.bio: The great synthesizer of the theosis tradition. His cosmic vision of five divisions overcome by Christ represents the fullest patristic theology of divinization.

authors.name: Cyril of Alexandria
authors.bio: Patriarch of Alexandria and architect of the Christological settlement at Ephesus (431). His Commentary on John contains important theosis passages.

authors.name: John of Damascus
authors.bio: The last of the great Eastern Church Fathers. His Exposition of the Orthodox Faith is the final systematic summary of patristic theology.

authors.name: Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite
authors.bio: Anonymous mystical theologian whose works, attributed to Paul's Athenian convert, defined the vocabulary of Christian mysticism including the term theosis itself.

authors.name: Augustine of Hippo
authors.bio: The most influential theologian in Western Christianity. Affirmed theosis but with important qualifications that shaped the Western tradition's more cautious approach to divinization.

authors.name: Valentinus
authors.bio: The most influential Gnostic teacher. Founded the Valentinian school, which developed the most elaborate cosmological system in Gnostic thought.

authors.name: Unknown (Gospel of Thomas)
authors.bio: The Gospel of Thomas is a collection of 114 sayings attributed to Jesus, preserved in Coptic in the Nag Hammadi library. Contains some of the most radical sayings on the divine within.

authors.name: Anonymous (Sumerian)
authors.bio: The Instructions of Shuruppak is one of the oldest surviving literary texts, a father's advice to his son from the earliest period of Sumerian civilization.

authors.name: Anonymous (Babylonian)
authors.bio: The Enuma Elish is the Babylonian creation epic, recited at the New Year festival. It describes the creation of humanity from the blood of a slain god.

authors.name: Anonymous (Egyptian)
authors.bio: The Book of the Dead is a collection of funerary spells and declarations, placed in tombs to guide the deceased through the afterlife.

authors.name: Hammurabi
authors.bio: King of Babylon. The Code of Hammurabi is one of the oldest known legal codes, with a prologue establishing the king's divine mandate.

authors.name: Akhenaten (attributed)
authors.bio: The pharaoh who introduced monotheistic worship of the Aten (sun disk). The Great Hymn to the Aten is the earliest known monotheistic text.

authors.name: Gregory Palamas
authors.bio: The theologian of the divine energies. His Triads defend the Hesychast monks and distinguish between God’s unknowable essence and his participable energies.

authors.name: John Chrysostom
authors.bio: The Golden Mouth. Archbishop of Constantinople, the greatest preacher of the early Church.

authors.name: Ephrem the Syrian
authors.bio: The greatest poet-theologian of the Syriac tradition.

authors.name: Pope Leo the Great
authors.bio: Pope who defined Christology at Chalcedon.

authors.name: Theophilus of Antioch
authors.bio: Bishop of Antioch, earliest to use the word ‘Trinity’.

authors.name: Justin Martyr
authors.bio: The first great Christian apologist.

authors.name: Methodius of Olympus
authors.bio: Bishop and martyr who defended bodily resurrection.

authors.name: Hilary of Poitiers
authors.bio: The Athanasius of the West. Defender of Nicene orthodoxy in Gaul.

authors.name: Ambrose of Milan
authors.bio: Bishop of Milan, mentor of Augustine.

authors.name: Symeon the New Theologian
authors.bio: The greatest mystical poet of Byzantium.

authors.name: Thomas Aquinas
authors.bio: The Angelic Doctor. Synthesized Aristotelian philosophy with Christian theology.

authors.name: Ignatius of Antioch
authors.bio: Martyr-bishop. His letters are among the earliest post-apostolic writings.

authors.name: Author of the Letter to Diognetus
authors.bio: Anonymous early Christian apologist.

authors.name: Author of the Epistle of Barnabas
authors.bio: Anonymous early Christian writer, distinct from the apostle Barnabas.

authors.name: Pseudo-Macarius
authors.bio: Anonymous author of the Macarian Homilies, foundational texts of Eastern Christian mysticism.

authors.name: Vedic Rishis
authors.bio: The seers (rishis) of the Vedic hymns, traditionally regarded as receivers of divine revelation rather than authors.

authors.name: Upanishadic Sages
authors.bio: The anonymous and named sages of the Upanishads, who developed the philosophical core of Hindu thought: Brahman, Atman, and their identity.

authors.name: Vyasa (attributed)
authors.bio: The legendary compiler of the Mahabharata, within which the Bhagavad Gita is set. Krishna's teachings on the divine Self.

authors.name: Patanjali
authors.bio: Compiler of the Yoga Sutras, the foundational text of yoga philosophy. Defines yoga as union of individual consciousness with the universal.

authors.name: Gautama Buddha (attributed)
authors.bio: The historical Buddha. His teachings on the nature of mind, suffering, and liberation form the foundation of Buddhism.

authors.name: Nagarjuna (attributed)
authors.bio: The founder of Madhyamaka philosophy. The Heart Sutra and Prajnaparamita literature are associated with his school.

authors.name: Huineng
authors.bio: The Sixth Patriarch of Chan (Zen) Buddhism. His Platform Sutra teaches that original nature is inherently pure.

authors.name: Hermes Trismegistus (attributed)
authors.bio: The legendary author of the Hermetic corpus. A syncretic figure combining the Egyptian god Thoth with the Greek god Hermes.

authors.name: Plotinus
authors.bio: The founder of Neoplatonism. His Enneads describe the emanation of reality from the One and the soul's return through mystical union.

authors.name: Proclus
authors.bio: The last great systematic Neoplatonist. His Elements of Theology formalized the structure of divine emanation.

authors.name: Porphyry
authors.bio: Student of Plotinus and editor of the Enneads. His own works develop the practical path of the soul's return to the divine.

authors.name: Anonymous (Norse)
authors.bio: The anonymous poets of the Poetic Edda, preserving pre-Christian Norse cosmology and mythology.

authors.name: Snorri Sturluson
authors.bio: Icelandic historian who compiled the Prose Edda, preserving Norse mythology in systematic form.

authors.name: Nagasena (attributed)
authors.bio: The Buddhist monk who debates King Milinda (Menander I) on the nature of the self.

authors.name: Wumen Huikai
authors.bio: Compiler of the Gateless Gate (Mumonkan), the most famous collection of Zen koans.
